html:
AQA GCSE English Literature — Worlds and Lives Anthology — 10 Most Likely Comparison Questions to Come Up (2026)

AQA English Literature Paper 2 — Section B (Anthology Poetry) Q24. 30 marks. Recommended writing time: 45 minutes. AOs: AO1 (response + textual reference) / AO2 (writer's methods) / AO3 (context). AQA prints ONE named anchor poem from the 15 in full on the paper; the student selects ONE OTHER poem from the anthology to compare. Stem reads: "Compare how poets present [THEME] in '[NAMED ANCHOR POEM]' and in one other poem from 'Worlds and lives'." — AQA supplies the named poem; the student picks the comparison.

How this list was generated

This Q-bank was built using Sophicly's AQA prediction algorithm adapted for the Worlds and Lives cluster's new-spec status (see `Worlds-and-Lives-AQA-Prediction-Algorithm.md` in this folder). The cluster was added to the AQA spec in 2023 and first examined in summer 2024, leaving only two live papers (2024 + 2025) — both with LOW triangulation confidence because AQA filestore returns image-only PDFs that no extractable-text method has yet recovered. The algorithm therefore swaps the per-text history-driven signals for the new-spec substitute-scoring approach: SpecAOWeight (0.30), SpecimenPaperMatch (0.35), AnalogousTextPattern against Power and Conflict (0.15), SiblingBoardSignal (0.10), and CoreTopicBoost (0.10) for the cluster's five core poems — Lines Written in Early Spring (Wordsworth — Romantic-roots), In a London Drawingroom (Eliot — Victorian-roots), A Portable Paradise (Robinson — diaspora-archive), Thirteen (Femi — race / state-contact), and With Birds You're Never Lonely (Antrobus — contemporary climate / non-human-consciousness). RecencyPenalty is light in a two-paper window but still active.

Section IDs (Q1–Q10) ARE the priority order. Q1 = highest-confidence anchor, Q10 = lowest within the top 10. Confidence labels in section headers are derived from the algorithm's substitute-scoring output, not from observed return cycles (which the cluster's thin history does not yet support).

Priority order — predicted 2026 named-anchor rankings

This table ranks our predictions for which Worlds and Lives poem AQA will print as the named anchor in 2026. The Q-bank below rehearses Sophicly's suggested comparison for each predicted anchor, but the student is always free to substitute a different second poem from the anthology.

| Algorithm rank | Predicted named anchor (AQA prints in full) | Confidence | Likely 2026 controlling theme | Sophicly's suggested comparison poem |

|---:|---|---|---|---|

| 1 | A Portable Paradise (Robinson, 2019) | HIGH | Belonging / inherited home | Homing (Berry) |

| 2 | Thirteen (Femi, 2020) | HIGH | Race / state contact / urban Black-British identity | On an Afternoon Train (Berry) |

| 3 | Lines Written in Early Spring (Wordsworth, 1798) | HIGH | Place / nature as moral teacher | In a London Drawingroom (Eliot) |

| 4 | In a London Drawingroom (Eliot, 1869) | HIGH | Urban experience / refusal of nature | Lines Written in Early Spring (Wordsworth) |

| 5 | With Birds You're Never Lonely (Antrobus, 2019) | MED-HIGH | Loneliness / non-human consciousness | Lines Written in Early Spring (Wordsworth) |

| 6 | Homing (Berry, 2014) | MED-HIGH | Language / inheritance / voice | A Portable Paradise (Robinson) |

| 7 | Like an Heiress (Nichols, 2020) | MED | Climate / Caribbean inheritance | A Portable Paradise (Robinson) |

| 8 | England in 1819 (Shelley, 1819) | MED | Power / state failure | Thirteen (Femi) |

| 9 | Name Journeys (Mundair, 2003) | MED | Identity / language reclamation | Homing (Berry) |

| 10 | On an Afternoon Train (Berry, 1995) | MED | Migration / cross-racial recognition | A Portable Paradise (Robinson) |

Coverage Pack (rehearse but not top-10):

• A century later (Dharker) — strong on conflict / generation themes; Malala intertext gives standalone weight.

• A Wider View (Seneviratne) — strong on family / labour history; Marshall's Temple Mill specificity.

• The Jewellery Maker (Parker) — strong on craft / rural Black presence.

• pot (Khan) — strong on object-poetics / postcolonial museum critique.

• Shall earth no more inspire thee (Brontë) — strong on place-as-speaker apostrophe.

How to use this document

How the AQA stem works. AQA Paper 2 Section B Q24 names ONE poem from the 15-poem anthology in the question stem and prints that poem in full on the exam paper. The student then selects ONE OTHER poem from the anthology — from memory — to compare. The stem is fixed in shape: "Compare how poets present [THEME] in '[NAMED ANCHOR POEM]' and in one other poem from 'Worlds and lives'. [30 marks]". AQA supplies the named poem and the controlling theme; the student picks the comparison.

What this Q-bank predicts. Each of the 10 questions below predicts the LIKELY named anchor for 2026 and pairs it with Sophicly's recommended comparison poem — the strongest defensible pairing under the predicted theme. The recommended comparison is NOT what AQA pairs (AQA pairs nothing; the student chooses) — it is the comparison we judge most rewarding for that anchor under that theme.

Each Q prints:

• The AQA-format stem with the predicted named anchor and predicted controlling theme.

• A Suggested comparison poem line — Sophicly's primary recommendation with a one-line rationale.

• An Alternative comparison candidates line — two or three other defensible pairings the student may prefer.

• A frame paragraph, a technique gloss, and a skeleton plan in Sophicly's locked comparison structure (Introduction 3 sentences / three Body Paragraphs of 7 sentences / Conclusion 4 sentences) adapted for poetry comparison.

Plan structure. Each plan follows AQA's correct integrated-comparison shape for anthology poetry: BP1 = FORM comparison (both poems woven together), BP2 = STRUCTURE comparison (both woven), BP3 = LANGUAGE comparison (both woven). Every BP uses Comparative TTECEA+C — every sentence compares both poems. If the student substitutes a different comparison from the alternative-candidates list, the form / structure / language axis stays; only the specific techniques and evidence change.

Rehearsal priority. If exam time is tight, rehearse the top 5 first (Q1–Q5). For comprehensive coverage, rehearse all 10. Memorise the cross-question reuse table at the end so the same prepared plans answer multiple Qs.

Pedagogical depth — three non-negotiables for Worlds and Lives

Every Grade-9 essay on a W&L comparison should thread at least one of these moves into one BP or the conclusion. They are the anthology's deepest analytical pivots; Sophia teaches them once so students can deploy them across any Q.

1. Identity is NOT a destination — it is an INTERNAL ARCHIVE carried. The anthology's deepest editorial argument.

The standard reading of Worlds and Lives treats the anthology as a comparison-set on identity, place, heritage, and diaspora. The sharper second reading exposes the anthology's deepest editorial argument: identity is not a destination but an INTERNAL ARCHIVE CARRIED. The teaching step is to replace place-as-identity with archive-as-identity. Robinson's A Portable Paradise (2019) names this most clearly: paradise is the inheritance the grandmother instructs the speaker to "carry it always / on my person, concealed"; the closing instruction "Shine the lamp on it like the fresh hope / of morning" names the inheritance as a portable practice, not a recoverable location. Femi's Thirteen (2020) locates the Black-British boy's interior — the "warmth of your teeth" held in a stop, the silent recognition that the same officer who once called the class "supernovas" now sees the boy as a "description of a man" — as the archive on which survival depends; identity is the speaker's interior management of a hostile public, not a Peckham address. Berry's Homing (2014) carries the Black Country dialect "in a box beneath the bed" until the closing "send your words, like pigeons, / fluttering for home" returns the inheritance through the body. Mundair's Name Journeys (2003), Khan's pot (postcolonial museum critique), and Antrobus's With Birds You're Never Lonely (deaf attention) all locate heritage as something INSIDE the speaker, not outside. The anthology's editorial work is to replace the fixed citizenship-document picture of belonging with the active practice-and-inheritance picture: paradise is not WHERE you are; it is WHAT YOU CARRY. Thread this into: Q1 A Portable Paradise / Q2 Thirteen / Q6 Homing / Q7 Like an Heiress / Q9 Name Journeys / Q10 On an Afternoon Train / any conclusion that names the anthology's contribution to contemporary British identity-debate.

2. The Romantic-roots poems anticipate the contemporary speakers — British poetry has been processing displacement long before twentieth-century migration.

The standard reading splits the anthology into a Romantic-roots half (Wordsworth, Brontë, Shelley, Eliot) and a contemporary half (Robinson, Femi, Berry, Mundair, Antrobus, Khan, Nichols, Seneviratne, Parker, Dharker). The sharper second reading exposes the editorial argument: the Romantic-roots poems present THE DISPOSSESSED OBSERVER as the precursor to the contemporary diaspora speaker. Wordsworth's 1798 grove in Lines Written in Early Spring — "To her fair works did Nature link / The human soul that through me ran" — places the speaker outside the political nation, working through displacement-from-the-inherited-order by means of nature. Eliot's In a London Drawingroom (1869) locates the Victorian woman as the dispossessed observer behind glass — "the world seems one huge prison-house" — whose vantage point anticipates the displacement contemporary speakers will name more openly. Shelley's England in 1819 indicts the Regency state as a system that has exiled its own people through "old, mad, blind, despised, and dying" rule. Brontë's Shall earth no more inspire thee (apostrophe — the personified earth addresses the wandering soul) locates the wanderer outside the home the earth claims. The Romantic-roots half therefore does not merely contrast with the contemporary half; it ANTICIPATES the contemporary speakers' structural position. British poetry has been processing displacement since the late eighteenth century — the diaspora speaker's experience extends a tradition the dominant culture had previously read as Romantic interiority. The anthology's historical span argues that displacement is not unique to twentieth-century migration but is the through-line of British lyric across its modern period. Thread this into: Q3 Lines Written in Early Spring / Q4 In a London Drawingroom / Q8 England in 1819 / any BP3 that pairs a Romantic-roots poem with a contemporary poem (e.g. Wordsworth with Antrobus, Eliot with Femi).

3. Name WHICH world, name WHICH life — the analytical precondition.

The anthology's title is plural on both sides — Worlds and Lives — and the editorial argument is that there is no single British world and no single British life. The anthology's worlds include the Romantic nation (Wordsworth's 1798 grove, Shelley's 1819 Regency state), the Victorian city (Eliot's London drawing-room), the post-Windrush commuter route (Berry's 1955 train), the diaspora interior (Robinson's pocket-paradise), the Black-British urban estate (Femi's Peckham), the colonised place re-claimed (Khan's pot), the climate-vulnerable coast (Nichols's Atlantic Guyana), the deaf-attentive sanctuary (Antrobus's Zealandia). The lives the anthology examines are the identities formed across these worlds: the Caribbean grandmother's heir, the bilingual British-Punjabi child, the Black-British thirteen-year-old, the Black-British rural Jewellery maker, the deaf Jamaican-British poet. Every comparison essay should name in its thesis WHICH world and WHICH life each poem inhabits and HOW the two worlds and the two lives produce different angles on the main idea. Generic "identity and belonging" answers cap at Band 4. Top-band answers specify and distinguish. Hall's "positioning" frame (1990) and Gilroy's Black Atlantic frame (1993) travel cleanly across the anthology's contemporary half; Robinson's "internal archive" is the anthology's own version of the same step.

Why this depth matters pedagogically. AQA's Band 6 descriptor asks for "a convincing, critical analysis and exploration" of method, sustained across the comparison. A reading that names archive-as-identity (not place-as-identity), names the Romantic-roots half as the contemporary speakers' precursor, and specifies WHICH world and WHICH life is the kind of thinking Examiner Reports name as the top-band signature. Without it, a comparison essay is parallel description; with it, the essay is critical analysis.

A universal method for finding the second reading. Every text in the AQA specification rewards the same approach: identify the standard reading; then identify a second reading the same evidence supports; then read the two together. For Worlds and Lives the paired readings are place-as-identity/archive-as-identity, Romantic-roots-vs-contemporary/Romantic-roots-as-contemporary-precursor, and generic-identity/specified-world-and-life. Sophia should generate the second reading whenever helping a student plan — the standard reading is what most students will write; the second reading is what carries them past the band-9 line.

The seven-sentence body paragraph shape — AQA integrated-comparison shape for W&L

BP1 = FORM comparison (both poems woven together). BP2 = STRUCTURE comparison (both woven). BP3 = LANGUAGE comparison (both woven). Every BP uses Comparative TTECEA+C — every element compares both poems within the same comparative step. Within each BP:

1. Comparative topic sentence — concept-led; explicit "While X..., Y..." contrast naming the form / structure / language axis. No techniques named yet.

2. Comparative technique — the form-related, structure-related, or language-related techniques in BOTH poems, named with sophisticated literary terms (free indirect discourse, anaphora, apostrophe, dramatic monologue, blank verse, code-switching, second-person address, in medias res, praeteritio, pathetic fallacy).

3. Comparative evidence — anchor quotes from BOTH poems held in the same step.

4. Comparative close analysis — word-level analysis on specific verbs / nouns / images from both anchor quotes, weaving the two.

5. Comparative effects — what the reader experiences in each poem, compared. Use specific emotion words (recognition, complicity, suffocation, satisfaction, indictment, tenderness, exposure, integration). Avoid generic "sadness" / "interest".

6. Comparative author's purpose — why each poet made that form / structure / language choice, compared. Hedged ("may have chosen", "can be read as choosing", "a defensible reading is", "perhaps").

7. Comparative context (AO3) — period-specific historical / cultural / institutional facts for both poets, compared. Each context drives that poet's specific choice (context shapes concept which produces method).

Memorise these once

The Worlds and Lives anthology argues that identity and belonging are worked out in the spaces between worlds — geographic, generational, linguistic, historical, and ecological — and that the question of who counts as British, who can speak in English, and whose worlds matter is reopened in each of fifteen poems from a different angle. Across poems written between 1798 and 2020, the anthology traces this argument from the Romantic criticism of the British political and natural order (Wordsworth, Shelley, Brontë) through the Victorian study of urban refusal (Eliot) into post-Windrush diasporic recognition (Berry's 1955 train) and finally into the contemporary reopening of belonging through dialect (Berry's Homing), bilingual inheritance (Mundair), object-poetics of colonial extraction (Khan), the family archive (Seneviratne), the schoolroom under threat (Dharker), rural craft (Parker), deaf attention (Antrobus), the grandmother's instruction (Robinson), the climate-vulnerable coast (Nichols), and the racialised state encounter (Femi). Two main tensions run through the anthology: SCALE (whose world the speaker inhabits and which life that world produces) and TIME (whether the inherited place must be returned to or whether it travels with the speaker as portable internal archive). Every Grade-9 comparison should hold both.

Key terms (memorise once — useful across multiple Qs):

• Apostrophe — direct address to an absent or non-human addressee (Brontë's Shall earth no more inspire thee gives the personified earth a speaking voice that addresses the wandering soul; Khan's pot features a diasporic-British speaker addressing the silent Nigerian museum pot directly).

• Code-switching — alternation between two languages or registers inside a single text (Berry's Black Country dialect inside standard-English syntax; Mundair's Punjabi-into-English).

• Blank verse — unrhymed iambic pentameter; the form Milton and Wordsworth used for epic and philosophical content (Eliot deploys it for a confined drawing-room — the mismatch is the argument).

• Polysyndetic / asyndetic catalogue — accumulation of items with / without conjunctions (Shelley's "old, mad, blind, despised, and dying" piles negative adjectives without coordinated relief).

• Pathetic fallacy — natural phenomena attributed human emotion (Wordsworth's flowers that "enjoy the air"; Brontë's personified earth that addresses the wandering soul).

• In medias res — opening mid-action without scene-setting (Femi's "You will be four minutes from home / when you are cornered by an officer").

• Second-person future address — speaker addresses the reader as a "you" the future will overtake (Femi's "You will be patted on the shoulder, then, by another fed"; Berry's grandmother spoken to as "you").

• Free indirect discourse — third-person narration that holds the character's voice without quotation marks (Seneviratne's "craving the comfort of a wider view").

• Praeteritio — Latin for saying something by saying you will not say it (Shelley's "I shall not catalogue all our failures" job performed by the sonnet's catalogue itself).

• Dramatic monologue — verse spoken by a constructed persona whose worldview the form exposes (Brontë's personified earth as speaker; Khan's speaker addressing the silent pot).

Cluster-specific framing (memorise once — threads into every W&L BP):

• The anthology's editorial argument — the anthology was built to widen which voices count as British literary speech. Placing James Berry's Caribbean voice next to Wordsworth's Romantic blank verse, Caleb Femi's Peckham boyhood next to Emily Brontë's Yorkshire moors, or Shamshad Khan's Manchester-Asian object lyric next to Roger Robinson's Trinidadian portable-paradise sermon, is itself a contextual factor the essay can write about.

• Hall and Gilroy on diaspora positioning — identity is "always a positioning" (Hall 1990); Black-British culture is fundamentally "Atlantic and transnational" (Gilroy 1993). Use sparingly in BP3 or conclusion of any contemporary-half comparison.

• Robinson's "internal archive" — heritage is an internal archive carried, not a destination. Read contemporary poems' identity politics through this lens.

• The Romantic criticism of authority — Wordsworth and Shelley wrote during the half-century after the French Revolution in which inherited authority was being stripped of legitimacy. Their poems share a refusal to accept that any inherited structure (monarchy, urban order, parliamentary settlement) is permanent or innocent.

• Post-Windrush British identity reopening — the 2017–18 Windrush scandal, the 2020 Black Lives Matter UK mobilisation, the post-2016 EU referendum debate on British identity, and the contemporary museum-decolonisation debate all sit inside the anthology's editorial moment.

---

Q1 — A Portable Paradise (predicted named anchor) [HIGH confidence — core poem · diaspora-archive + grandmother-inheritance]

Extract

Anchor poem (printed on the AQA paper):

And if I speak of Paradise,

then I'm speaking of my grandmother

who told me to carry it always

on my person, concealed, so

no one else would know but me.

That way they can't steal it, she'd say.

And if life puts you under pressure,

trace its ridges in your pocket,

smell its piney scent on your handkerchief,

hum its anthem under your breath.

And if your stresses are sustained and daily,

get yourself to an empty room – be it hotel,

hostel or hovel – find a lamp

and empty your paradise onto a desk:

your white sands, green hills and fresh fish.

Shine the lamp on it like the fresh hope

of morning, and keep staring at it till you sleep.

— Roger Robinson, A Portable Paradise (2019)

Suggested comparison poem (student choice — pick any other Worlds and Lives poem): Homing by Liz Berry. Why: matches the grandmother-as-inheritance-source angle this Q rewards, and pairs the diaspora-archive (Robinson) with the dialect-archive (Berry) — both inheritances matrilineal, both portable, both refusing the picture of belonging as passport or birth certificate.

Alternative comparison candidates:

• Name Journeys (Mundair) — bilingual interior carried as identity-archive; same internal-archive logic via Punjabi/English doubling.

• Like an Heiress (Nichols) — Caribbean coastal inheritance walked rather than carried; same Atlantic-archive frame under different contemporary pressure.

• On an Afternoon Train (Berry) — dignity in the diaspora as small public choice; Caribbean inheritance held against the British public sphere.

Question

Compare how poets present ideas about belonging in 'A Portable Paradise' and in one other poem from 'Worlds and lives'. [30 marks]

Frame

Robinson's 2019 stanzaic meditation and Berry's 2014 dialect-recovery lyric both present belonging as inheritance from a grandmother figure carried into the speaker's present, but their inheritances differ in tone: Robinson's grandmother gives a meditative practice ("Shine the lamp on it like the fresh hope / of morning"), while Berry's grandmother gives a stigmatised dialect ("kept your accent / in a box beneath the bed"). The plan traces three layers of the comparison — form (unbroken imperative-chain instruction versus bilingual free verse), structure (unbroken transmission versus loss-and-return), and language (the lamp-and-fresh-hope simile versus the homing-pigeon simile).

Pedagogical depth to thread. Name WHICH world, name WHICH life. Robinson's world is the Trinidadian-British diaspora interior carried on the person, concealed; the life is the diasporic adult honouring grandmother-wisdom across the Middle Passage. Berry's world is the Black Country dialect community before and after the schoolroom; the life is the working-class woman re-claiming her grandmother's voice after institutional suppression. Both inheritances are passed down through the mother's line and portable rather than national and fixed — and the anthology's editorial argument that belonging is plural rather than centred runs through both poems. Land this in BP3 or the conclusion. Hall's "positioning" frame works cleanly here.

Technique gloss:

• Stanzaic free verse with sustained imperative chain (Robinson) — instruction delivered across short stanzaic units (one-, two-, four-line groupings) without break in the imperative chain.

• Imperative mood (Robinson) — "trace", "smell", "hum", "Shine" — direct instruction to the speaker and through him to the reader.

• Closing simile (Robinson) — "Shine the lamp on it like the fresh hope / of morning" — light cast on the inherited image at the end of the day.

• Bilingual free verse (Berry) — Black Country dialect words ("bibble, fittle, tay, wum") integrated unitalicised inside standard-English syntax.

• Metallurgical metaphor (Berry) — "I wanted to forge your voice / in my mouth, a blacksmith's furnace" — voice as ironworking labour.

• Closing homing-pigeon simile (Berry) — "send your words, like pigeons, / fluttering for home" — the inherited words flying back the way pigeons home to a working-class loft.

• Hook (historical fact tied to thesis): Roger Robinson was writing in 2019 from a Trinidadian-British diaspora reckoning with the 2017–18 Windrush scandal, in which Caribbean-British citizens who had arrived as children on the MV Empire Windrush and its successor ships had been wrongly reclassified as illegal immigrants and in some cases deported by their own government. Liz Berry, writing in 2014 from a Black Country whose dialect had been suppressed across a century of state schoolroom drilling — Received Pronunciation promoted as the prestige variety from the late-Victorian elementary-school settlement onward — addressed a region whose vowel quality had been most stigmatised by the long twentieth-century elocution drilling that trained working-class regional voices out of state-school children.

• Building sentence (anthology context only): The piece appeared in his T. S. Eliot-Prize-winning A Portable Paradise (2019), composed in the immediate shadow of the 2017 Grenfell Tower fire that had killed seventy-two people, most of them Black and minority-ethnic Londoners whom his earlier reportage as Trinidadian-British performance poet had documented. Behind Berry's piece stood her own Black Country childhood, in which she had been taught to recognise her own grandmother's homing-pigeon dialect as the unprestigious variant the schoolroom had been built to remove, and the post-2000 revival of regional voice in British poetry through figures such as Kei Miller and Daljit Nagra.

• Thesis: Across form (free verse with unbroken imperative chain versus bilingual free verse), structure (continuous transmission versus loss-and-return), and language (the lamp-and-fresh-hope simile versus the homing-pigeon simile), Robinson stages belonging as a meditative practice inherited from a grandmother and carried internally across the diaspora while Berry stages belonging as a stigmatised dialect reclaimed from a grandmother whose accent the schoolroom had locked in a box — both poems together arguing that the place a person belongs to is something a previous generation handed forward rather than something the citizenship register confirms.

• Comparative topic sentence: While Robinson chooses a stanzaic meditative free-verse form whose short stanzaic units sustain an unbroken imperative chain the diaspora preserved, Berry chooses a stanza-broken bilingual free-verse form to present belonging as a dialect the institution broke and the speaker must recover.

• Comparative technique: Robinson's form holds the shape of the grandmotherly counsel-poem as one continuous imperative-meditation chain held across short stanzaic units, whereas Berry's form holds the shape of the bilingual elegy inside stanzas that absorb Black Country dialect words unitalicised inside standard-English syntax.

• Comparative evidence: Robinson opens "And if I speak of Paradise, / then I'm speaking of my grandmother" and closes "Shine the lamp on it like the fresh hope / of morning, and keep staring at it till you sleep" across stanzas held as one continuous imperative chain across the poem's short stanzaic units, while Berry opens "For years you kept your accent / in a box beneath the bed" and breaks into the closing pigeon simile "send your words, like pigeons, / fluttering for home" across stanzas that name suppression and return.

• Comparative close analysis: Robinson's choice of the counsel-poem form makes the unbroken imperative chain across stanzas the argument — the grandmother's voice continues as a single piece of instruction the diaspora preserved — while Berry's choice of the bilingual-elegy form makes the stanza breaks themselves an argument, each break marking a phase (suppression, leakage, recovery) the dialect had to survive.

• Comparative effects: Thus the reader of Robinson follows the imperative chain "trace… smell… hum… Shine" as one continuous instruction, while the reader of Berry sees the closing image of pigeons fluttering home after years inside the box — Robinson's continuous instruction is set against Berry's broken-and-returning flight.

• Comparative author's purpose: Robinson may have chosen the meditative imperative-chain form to argue that diasporic inheritance is a practice the speaker cannot afford to interrupt, while Berry can be read as choosing the bilingual-elegy form to argue that the institutional suppression of dialect must be named through the stanza-architecture itself before the recovery can land.

• Comparative context: Robinson writes inside the long Caribbean tradition of grandmother-wisdom poetry — Grace Nichols's I Coming Back, James Berry's Lucy's Letters, Olive Senior's Talking of Trees — that treats the elderly woman as the main carrier of cultural inheritance across the Middle Passage, while Berry writes inside the contemporary recovery of regional voice that early-twentieth-century elocution drilling and the long state-school promotion of Received Pronunciation had clamped out of Black Country children across her grandmother's generation.

• Comparative topic sentence: While Robinson structures the poem as one continuous imperative transmission moving from grandmother to speaker to reader, Berry structures the poem as a phased loss-and-return moving from suppression through leakage to recovery.

• Comparative technique: Robinson deploys cumulative imperative parallelism ("trace its ridges… smell its piney scent… hum its anthem… Shine the lamp") sustained across stanzas inside a single continuous instruction, while Berry deploys a three-phase narrative arc — accent locked away, accent leaked, accent reclaimed — patterned through stanza-breaks that mark each shift.

• Comparative evidence: Robinson's structural backbone runs "trace its ridges in your pocket" through "empty your paradise onto a desk: / your white sands, green hills and fresh fish" to "Shine the lamp on it like the fresh hope / of morning, and keep staring at it till you sleep", whereas Berry's backbone moves from "in a box beneath the bed" through "We heard it escape sometimes" to "I wanted to forge your voice / in my mouth" across the poem's five stanzas.

• Comparative close analysis: Robinson's parallel imperative chain makes the structural continuity the argument — the inheritance is one piece of practised instruction running from grandmother through speaker to reader — while Berry's three-phase arc makes the stanza-breaks the argument, the gaps between stanzas standing for the years during which the dialect was institutionally clamped.

• Comparative effects: Indeed Robinson's continuous instruction moves the imperatives "trace", "smell", "hum", "Shine" forward in one cumulative current, while Berry's stanza breaks mark each phase the voice has had to pass through — "in a box beneath the bed" (suppression), "We heard it escape sometimes" (leakage), "send your words, like pigeons, / fluttering for home" (recovery). Robinson's continuous run is set against Berry's three-stage phased shape.

• Comparative author's purpose: Robinson can be read as structuring the poem to argue that interior practice is one of the resources British state failure cannot reach, while Berry may have been structuring the poem to argue that recovered voice carries the historical mark of the institution it had to survive.

• Comparative context: Robinson writes inside the post-Grenfell 2019 recognition that the British state had visibly failed Black Londoners — the seventy-two dead in the 2017 council-housing fire, the 2017-18 Windrush scandal — while Berry writes inside the long twentieth-century schoolroom suppression of regional dialects (Black Country, Geordie, Scouse) under the systematic state-school promotion of Received Pronunciation as the prestige variety, the institutional drilling reaching back through her grandmother's early-twentieth-century elementary-school years.

• Comparative topic sentence: While Robinson centres his closing argument on a lamp-and-fresh-hope simile that pictures belonging as an inherited image lit in the privacy of a hired room, Berry centres her closing argument on a homing-pigeon simile that pictures belonging as an instinctual flight the inherited words make through the public air.

• Comparative technique: Robinson layers imperative verbs ("trace", "smell", "hum", "Shine") with the concrete domestic catalogue "hotel, / hostel or hovel" and the closing dawn simile, while Berry layers metallurgical lexis ("ferrous", "coal", "blacksmith's furnace") with dialect lexis ("bibble, fittle, tay, wum") absorbed unitalicised inside standard-English syntax.

• Comparative evidence: Robinson closes "Shine the lamp on it like the fresh hope / of morning, and keep staring at it till you sleep", while Berry closes "send your words, like pigeons, / fluttering for home" and earlier embeds "vowels ferrous as nails, consonants / you could lick the coal from".

• Comparative close analysis: Robinson's imperative "Shine" and dawn-simile "like the fresh hope / of morning" together locate inheritance inside a centuries-old diasporic tradition of small lit-and-held inherited objects sustained against hostile public cultures, while Berry's pigeon image and metallurgical (metal-working) lexis locate inheritance inside Black Country industrial labour and working-class pigeon-fancying.

• Comparative effects: Therefore Robinson's closing simile "like the fresh hope / of morning" names a private practice carried out in a temporary room ("hotel, / hostel or hovel") where no public eye can intervene, while Berry's closing simile "like pigeons, / fluttering for home" names a bird-flock returning publicly through the air to a working-class loft. Robinson's private temporary-room ritual is set against Berry's visible homecoming flight.

• Comparative author's purpose: Robinson may have chosen the lamp-and-fresh-hope language to argue that diasporic dignity is practised privately because the public sphere cannot be trusted, while Berry can be read as choosing the homing-pigeon language to argue that working-class regional voice belongs in public English literary writing rather than as italicised curiosity.

• Comparative context: Robinson writes inside the long Caribbean tradition of the carried-and-lit diasporic figure — the inheritance the migrant carries on the body across the Atlantic — while Berry writes inside the Black Country tradition in which pigeon-fancying remains a working-class pastime and the dialect descends from Old English and West Mercian with significant pre-Norman retention.

What man has made of man?

Question

Compare how poets present ideas about place and nature in 'Lines Written in Early Spring' and in one other poem from 'Worlds and lives'. [30 marks]

Frame

Wordsworth's 1798 ballad-metre quatrains and Eliot's 1869 blank-verse anatomy both use a single national-political setting as the medium through which a structural argument about human living is delivered, but their conclusions diverge along the anthology's central country-and-city axis: Wordsworth's primrose grove invites moral questioning through what nature reveals about humanity, while Eliot's London drawing-room registers urban refusal through what the city blocks the speaker from seeing. The plan traces the difference between what the country includes and what the city refuses.

Pedagogical depth to thread. Both poems were written in moments of political pressure — Wordsworth at the end of the 1790s under the Combination Acts and post-revolutionary repression, Eliot in 1869 after the 1867 Second Reform Act and inside Britain's imperial-metropolitan moment. Both poets turn a single setting into a structural criticism of the whole British society around them. The anthology's editorial argument that nature poetry is never only nature poetry runs through both texts.

Technique gloss:

• Iambic tetrameter quatrains (Wordsworth) — folk-ballad metre married to philosophical content.

• Rhetorical question (Wordsworth) — "Have I not reason to lament / What man has made of man?" converts the poem from nature-lyric to political indictment.

• Pathetic fallacy (Wordsworth) — "every flower / Enjoys the air it breathes".

• Blank verse (Eliot) — unrhymed iambic pentameter, the form Milton and Wordsworth used for epic — deployed here for a confined interior.

• Negation cluster (Eliot) — "no", "nor", "not", "lowest rate" repeated as the poem's defining mode.

• Extended prison metaphor (Eliot) — "one huge prison-house & court".

• Hook (historical fact tied to thesis): William Wordsworth was writing in 1798 as a young Romantic from Alfoxden in the Quantocks, Somerset, inside a Britain whose Pitt administration was preparing the Combination Acts of 1799–1800 that would outlaw working-class political organisation, suspend habeas corpus, and criminalise the rural and urban assemblies his primrose-grove ethical question quietly indicts. George Eliot, writing in 1869 from her partner's Regent's Park home in the imperial-metropolitan London of post-Reform Britain, addressed a city in which the Second Reform Act's 1867 expansion of male suffrage had doubled the urban electorate without changing the conditions of moral life her drawing-room window framed.

• Building sentence (anthology context only): The piece appeared in the first edition of Lyrical Ballads — co-authored with Samuel Taylor Coleridge — the volume usually dated as the start of English Romanticism, and was drafted in the year before the Acts that confirmed the ethical question its closing line had refused to close. Behind Eliot's piece stood her fifteen-year unmarried partnership with the philosopher George Henry Lewes in The Priory at North Bank (their home since 1863) — a social outsider's London position that the smoke and grime of the imperial-metropolitan capital reframed as structural rather than personal.

• Thesis: Across form (folk-ballad quatrains versus blank verse), structure (sensory pleasure leading to moral questioning versus continuous accumulating negation refusing relief), and language (pantheist personification versus industrial-grey negation), Wordsworth stages place as the natural world's quiet indictment of "what man has made of man" while Eliot stages place as the urban world's structural refusal of colour, warmth, and joy — both poems together arguing that any honest attention to a national setting produces moral discomfort the surrounding political order would prefer to deny.

• Comparative topic sentence: While Wordsworth chooses iambic tetrameter quatrains in alternating ABAB rhyme to deliver structural criticism through a democratic folk-ballad form, Eliot chooses unrhymed iambic pentameter blank verse to deliver structural criticism through a form Milton and Wordsworth had used for epic.

• Comparative technique: Wordsworth works inside the Lyrical Ballads programme — folk-ballad metre married to philosophical content — whereas Eliot works inside the Victorian blank-verse meditation — the epic-philosophical form repurposed for a confined drawing-room interior.

• Comparative evidence: Wordsworth holds the form across six quatrains opening "I heard a thousand blended notes, / While in a grove I sate reclined" and closing "Have I not reason to lament / What man has made of man?", while Eliot holds her form across nineteen unrhymed lines opening "The sky is cloudy, yellowed by the smoke" and closing "With lowest rate of colour, warmth & joy".

• Comparative close analysis: Wordsworth's choice of folk-ballad form makes the regularity itself the argument — the closing question lands with the inescapability of hymn or nursery rhyme — while Eliot's choice of blank-verse form makes the form-content mismatch the argument, the epic measure deployed for the drawing-room making the room's airlessness more visible.

• Comparative effects: Thus Wordsworth's closing "Have I not reason to lament / What man has made of man?" rides ballad-metre into the reader's ear like a hymn line, while Eliot's nineteen unbroken lines have no white space until the closing full stop. Wordsworth's regular ABAB chime is set against Eliot's gap-less paragraph block.

• Comparative author's purpose: Wordsworth may have chosen the folk-ballad form to argue that radical political content reaches further inside the most democratic of English forms, while Eliot can be read as choosing the blank-verse form to argue that the Victorian compromise her speaker inhabits cannot be redeemed by even the most elevated literary tradition.

• Comparative context: Wordsworth writes inside the 1798 inauguration of English Romanticism (Lyrical Ballads published the same year with Coleridge), at a moment when the Combination Acts of 1799-1800 would outlaw working-class political organisation and the suspension of habeas corpus criminalise political dissent. Eliot writes in 1869 at The Priory in Regent's Park, three years after the 1867 Second Reform Act had partially expanded male suffrage but left the structural inequalities her drawing-room records untouched.

• Comparative topic sentence: While Wordsworth structures the poem as four quatrains of sensory pleasure turning in the final two quatrains into rhetorical-question indictment, Eliot structures the poem as nineteen lines of continuous accumulating negation that refuses any volta (turn) or relief.

• Comparative technique: Wordsworth uses a pleasure-to-question pivot — four observational quatrains of primrose, periwinkle, birds, and budding twigs collapsing into the closing rhetorical question — whereas Eliot uses continuous accumulating negation across an unbroken paragraph — "no", "nor", "not", "lowest rate" recurring without sectional break.

• Comparative evidence: Wordsworth structurally pivots from "Through primrose tufts, in that green bower, / The periwinkle trailed its wreaths" into "Have I not reason to lament / What man has made of man?", while Eliot accumulates from "No bird can make a shadow as it flies, / For all is shadow" into "The world seems one huge prison-house & court".

• Comparative close analysis: Wordsworth's pivot from pastoral observation to rhetorical question makes the structural pleasure-to-questioning movement the argument — pleasure honestly attended produces the indictment — while Eliot's continuous accumulating negation makes the absence of pivot the argument, the room's airlessness so total no formal turn can produce relief.

• Comparative effects: Indeed Wordsworth's four observational quatrains of "primrose tufts", "periwinkle", "birds", and "budding twigs" turn at quatrain five into the rhetorical question, while Eliot's repeated "no", "nor", "not", "lowest rate" recur straight through the paragraph without a single break. Wordsworth's quatrain-five turn is set against Eliot's unbroken chain of negatives.

• Comparative author's purpose: Wordsworth may have been structuring the poem to argue that the natural world's quiet abundance must be honestly attended before the political indictment can land, while Eliot can be read as structuring the poem to argue that the urban order's structural refusal of colour, warmth, and joy cannot be redeemed by any conventional formal turn.

• Comparative context: Wordsworth writes inside the Romantic pantheist inheritance that treats the natural world as morally continuous with the human, while Eliot writes inside the post-Reform Victorian recognition that imperial-metropolitan commerce had produced an interior airlessness the franchise extension of 1867 had not answered. In a London Drawingroom itself remained unpublished in Eliot's lifetime, surfacing in print only in 1959.

• Comparative topic sentence: While Wordsworth centres his closing argument on pantheist personification that gives the natural world a community of consciousness, Eliot centres her closing argument on industrial-grey negation that turns the natural canopy into prison architecture.

• Comparative technique: Wordsworth uses pathetic fallacy (natural events given human emotions) with the modal of compulsion — "every flower / Enjoys the air it breathes" alongside "And I must think, do all I can" — whereas Eliot uses smoke-and-shadow imagery with prison-house extended metaphor — "The sky is cloudy, yellowed by the smoke" alongside "one huge prison-house & court".

• Comparative evidence: Wordsworth's lexical signature runs "every flower / Enjoys the air it breathes" through "Their thoughts I cannot measure" to "What man has made of man", while Eliot's runs "For all is shadow" through "Monotony of surface & of form" to "lowest rate of colour, warmth & joy".

• Comparative effects: Therefore Wordsworth's "every flower / Enjoys the air it breathes" sets a primrose alongside a human speaker as if both share the same sentence, while Eliot's "lowest rate of colour, warmth & joy" runs "colour", "warmth", and "joy" through a noun ("rate") that belongs in a ledger or rent-book. • Comparative author's purpose: Wordsworth may have chosen the pantheist language to argue that natural abundance indicts the political order that has refused it to most humans, while Eliot can be read as choosing the industrial-grey language to name the moral cost of the Victorian compromise from inside the comfort that compromise produced.

• Comparative context: Wordsworth writes inside the late-1790s Romantic-pantheist inheritance shared with Coleridge's The Eolian Harp and the wider Lyrical Ballads project responding to French Revolution failure and Pittite repression, while Eliot writes inside the Victorian commercial-imperial vocabulary where book-keeping wording ("rate") had begun to take over the language of interior life under the pressure of industrial London.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Wordsworth and Eliot both turn a single national setting into structural criticism, but the country-and-city axis along which their settings stand produces opposite formal arcs: Wordsworth's ballad-metre quatrains earn moral questioning through pleasure, while Eliot's blank-verse paragraph refuses the relief any conventional turn would supply.

• Controlling concept: Place in the anthology is not a neutral background but the medium through which a structural argument about human living is delivered — and the rural-lyric and urban-lyric forms together stake the anthology's editorial claim that any honest attention to a national setting produces moral discomfort.

• Authors' purposes contrasted: Wordsworth may have been arguing that pleasure honestly attended to is itself a political indictment, while Eliot can be read as examining the moral cost of the Victorian compromise from inside the comfort that compromise produced — both poets refuse the consolation their respective settings might have offered.

• Ultimate moral about what comparing them reveals: Comparing the two poems reveals that the anthology's deepest argument on place is the recognition that the country and the city are different vehicles for the same indictment — the British political and urban orders have refused different parts of the same flourishing — and the lyric form is the wording in which the refusal is most precisely named.

Mundair's 2003 bilingual continuous-stanza lyric and Berry's 2014 dialect-recovery lyric both examine identity as the relationship between an inherited tongue and the institution that demanded assimilation. The plan traces the renaming-and-reclamation difference between a Punjabi child anglicised into the "Anglo Echo chamber" and a Black Country grandmother whose accent was trained out of the speaker's mouth.

• Comparative topic sentence: While Mundair chooses a bilingual continuous-stanza free-verse form that holds Hindu-epic intertext and migrant interior inside a single unbroken movement, Berry chooses a stanza-broken bilingual free-verse form that absorbs dialect words unitalicised inside paragraph-architecture.

• Comparative technique: Mundair works inside the postcolonial code-switching tradition — Hindu-epic intertext and Punjabi named directly inside English syntax, held inside a continuous stanza — whereas Berry works inside the bilingual-elegy tradition — Black Country dialect-into-English code-switching held inside stanza-broken free verse.

• Comparative evidence: Mundair opens "Like Rama I have felt the Wilderness" and closes the anchor extract on "my voice a mystery / in the Anglo Echo chamber – / void of history and memory", while Berry opens "For years you kept your accent / in a box beneath the bed" and closes "send your words, like pigeons, / fluttering for home".

• Comparative close analysis: Mundair's choice of continuous-stanza form makes the unbroken arc itself the argument — the violation and the intertextual self-positioning held in a single movement that refuses tidy resolution — while Berry's choice of bilingual-elegy form makes the unitalicised dialect words the argument, this style legitimate English literary writing rather than curiosity to be glossed.

Mundair's continuous bilingual movement is set against Berry's unmarked dialect words on the page.

• Comparative author's purpose: Mundair can be read as choosing the continuous-stanza form to argue that the bilingual mouth is doubled rather than divided and that the violation cannot be tidily compartmentalised, while Berry may have chosen the bilingual-elegy form to argue that the British literary tradition has been too narrow about which voices count as English literature.

• Comparative context: Mundair was born in Ludhiana in the Indian Punjab and raised in Manchester and Leicester after emigrating as a child in the 1970s — the poem is from her 2003 collection Lovers, Liars, Conjurers, and Thieves. Berry writes inside the post-1944-Education-Act recovery of Black Country dialect, which descends from Old English and West Mercian with significant pre-Norman retention.

• Comparative topic sentence: While Mundair structures the poem as a continuous arc moving from intertextual Hindu-epic self-positioning through the milk-teeth dislodgement of the Punjabi into the closing "Anglo Echo chamber", Berry structures the poem as a phased loss-and-return moving from suppression through leakage to recovery across stanzas.

• Comparative author's purpose: Mundair can be read as structuring the poem to argue that the bilingual mouth's experience is too continuous and unfinished to be tidily compartmentalised, while Berry may have been structuring the poem to argue that recovered voice carries the historical mark of the institution it had to survive.

• Comparative context: Mundair writes inside the post-Partition migration's bilingual interior and the mid-twentieth-century systemic anglicisation of South Asian children's names (Raymond for Raman, Susan for Sushma, assigned without consultation in schools and immigration offices). Berry writes inside the post-1944 educational settlement in which Received Pronunciation was promoted as the prestige variety and regional dialects trained out of working-class children.

• Comparative topic sentence: While Mundair centres her closing argument on a Hindu-epic intertext and botanical-soil image field that pictures the bilingual mouth as held between mythic inheritance and barren English ground, Berry centres her closing argument on the elocution-and-ruler image and the homing-pigeon simile that pictures the dialect as instinctual return after institutional clamping.

The piece by Robinson appeared in his T. S. Eliot-Prize-winning A Portable Paradise (2019), composed in the immediate shadow of the 2017 Grenfell Tower fire that had killed seventy-two people, most of them Black and minority-ethnic Londoners whose families his earlier reportage had documented at close range.

Reuse pattern. A Portable Paradise and Homing pair across Q1 and Q6 with different anchor focus (belonging in Q1, language in Q6). Lines Written in Early Spring and In a London Drawingroom pair across Q3 and Q4 (same pair, anchor reverses). Thirteen and On an Afternoon Train pair across Q2 and Q10 (same pair, anchor reverses). A Portable Paradise serves Q1 (anchor), Q6 (partner for Homing), Q7 (partner for Like an Heiress), and Q10 (partner for On an Afternoon Train) — four Qs from a single deeply-rehearsed plan. The student who rehearses these four pair-foundations (Robinson/Berry, Wordsworth/Eliot, Femi/Berry, Nichols/Robinson) plus the bilingual-reclamation foundation (Mundair/Berry) and the state-violence foundation (Shelley/Femi) and the deaf-poetics foundation (Antrobus/Wordsworth) covers the full top 10 with seven prepared plans.

Two critical voices to deploy

Sophicly deploys two named critical voices across the W&L corpus. Use them sparingly — once per essay maximum — and only when they sharpen the close analysis.

• Stuart Hall on diaspora and identity. Attribute as "Jamaican-British cultural theorist Stuart Hall" or simply "Hall". The reading: identity is "always a positioning" rather than a fixed essence inherited from a single point of origin (per his 1990 essay Cultural Identity and Diaspora); diasporic identity is "the experience of staying and the experience of leaving" carried simultaneously. Use in any Q where contemporary identity politics is the analytical event — particularly Q1, Q2, Q6, Q9, Q10.

• Paul Gilroy on the Black Atlantic and modernity. Attribute as "British cultural theorist Paul Gilroy" or simply "Gilroy". The reading: Black-British and Black-American culture are "constitutively Atlantic and transnational" rather than minor footnotes to European modernity (per his 1993 book The Black Atlantic: Modernity and Double Consciousness); the Atlantic crossing is the central modern experience. Use in any Q where the anthology's contemporary Black-British poetry is the focus — particularly Q1, Q2, Q7, Q10.

(Attribute readings to these named voices; do not name Mr Bruff in essays. Bruff is a teaching guide for revision, not an academic source for citation.)

Final reminder bullet

• Name WHICH world, name WHICH life. Worlds and Lives is plural on both sides; every comparison thesis must specify the world the speaker inhabits and the life that world produces. Generic answers cap at Band 4.

• Hall + Gilroy on diaspora positioning. Use sparingly in any contemporary-half comparison; once per essay maximum.

• Robinson's "internal archive" frame. Heritage is portable, inherited, and practised — not a destination but a discipline.

• One anchor quote per BP — single line, embodying the topic-sentence concept.

• Discourse markers when switching poets — Thus / Whereas / By contrast / However / Indeed / Similarly / In conclusion.

• Hedged author's-purpose claims — may have / can be read as / a defensible reading is.

• No "shows" as analytical verb — illustrates / portrays / registers / enacts / dramatises / exposes / reveals / conveys / mirrors / signals.

• No arrows — through / from / leading to / which produces.

• No feminist-critique framework — reframe gender as social order / honour culture / paternal authority / institutional practice.

• No explicit sexual references — audience is 13-16; reframe Victorian-era references in legal-status / social-vulnerability terms.

• No magic / spellbook framing — drop spellbook / magic / spell / incantation across all copy.

• No "patriarchy" / "patriarchal" — use society / social order / paternal / Elizabethan-or-Victorian substitutes.

• Academic register throughout — no contractions, no colloquialisms, no chatty asides, no exclamation marks outside quoted text.

• Hook must tie thesis — fact / quote / question / metaphor that encapsulates THIS essay's specific argument.

• Sentence cap ~35-45 words / ~3 lines. Split rather than truncate.

• Topic sentences are CONCEPT-FIRST — no techniques named in sentence 1; do not even name forms.
